confidence.disclosure: Nizamuddin Auliya is exceptionally well documented for a 13th-century Sufi because his disciple Amir Hasan Sijzi recorded his daily sayings in the Fawa'id al-Fu'ad — the first and most famous of the Indian Sufi malfuzat collections. We have his teaching, table conversation, and personal history in remarkable detail. His arrival in Delhi as a child, his training under Farid al-Din, his decades in Ghiyaspur, and his complex relationship with the Delhi Sultanate are all firmly attested.

sources: Bruce Lawrence (tr.), Nizam ad-Din Awliya: Morals for the Heart (1992)
Khaliq Ahmad Nizami, The Life and Times of Shaikh Nizamu'd-din Auliya (1991)

journey.event: Born in the city of Badaun in northern India in Safar 636 AH. His parents are Bukhari sayyids whose families had migrated from Central Asia to escape the Mongol invasions and settled in India under the early Delhi sultans.

Loses his father, the saintly Ahmad Bukhari. His mother Bibi Zulaikha raises him in great poverty in Badaun. He later remembers her saying, on nights when there was nothing to eat, 'Today we are guests of God.'
Memorises the Quran and begins formal study with the local scholars. He shows exceptional intelligence and is recognised as a prodigy by his teachers.
Moves with his mother and sister to Delhi for higher studies. He studies hadith, jurisprudence, and Arabic literature under the leading scholars of the capital.

Travels to Ajodhan (modern Pakpattan in Pakistan) to meet the great Chishti master Shaikh Farid al-Din Ganj-i-Shakar. He becomes Farid's disciple — Farid recognises him at first sight and tells him: 'You will be a tree under whose shade many will rest.'
Receives the khilafat — the formal succession — from Farid al-Din shortly before the master's death, becoming his principal spiritual heir.
Returns to Delhi after his master's death and begins to teach. He chooses to live not in the city itself but in the village of Ghiyaspur on the Yamuna, four miles from the walls of Delhi.

His khanqah at Ghiyaspur becomes the spiritual centre of north India. The community's langar (free kitchen) feeds hundreds daily. He refuses gifts from the sultans and accepts only voluntary offerings (futuh) from ordinary disciples.

Sultan Jalal al-Din Khalji invites him to court repeatedly. Nizamuddin refuses every invitation, telling messengers his door has two ways out and if the sultan comes in one he will go out the other. The principle of distance from political power becomes a Chishti hallmark.

Sultan Ala al-Din Khalji takes the throne. His relationship with Nizamuddin remains tense throughout his reign — the sultan wants the saint's blessing for his conquests, the saint refuses to grant it.

His most famous disciples gather around him: the poet and musician Amir Khusrau (whose Indo-Persian poetry will define north Indian Muslim culture), Amir Hasan Sijzi (his Boswell), and the future masters of the Chishti order Burhanuddin Gharib, Nasiruddin Chiragh, and others.

Sultan Ghiyas al-Din Tughluq, the new ruler of Delhi, demands a portion of the futuh donations the khanqah receives. Nizamuddin's reply, in the form of the famous prediction 'Hanuz Dilli dur ast' ('Delhi is still far away'), foretells that the sultan will not return alive. Tughluq dies in a falling pavilion at Afghanpur on his return from a campaign before reaching Delhi.

Amir Hasan Sijzi finishes the first volume of the Fawa'id al-Fu'ad, his record of Nizamuddin's daily teachings — the first malfuzat collection of Indian Sufism. Nizamuddin reads it, approves it, and the practice of recording a master's words in real time becomes standard.

Dies on 18 Rabi' al-Thani 725 AH (3 April 1325) in Ghiyaspur. He is buried at his khanqah; the dargah of Hazrat Nizamuddin in Delhi grows around his tomb and remains the most important Sufi shrine in India to this day, the heart of the Chishti world.
